metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.finding: The deletion log artifact path is never named, unlike the backup artifact which has an explicit path (`artifacts/jira-cleanup/backup-<ts>.json`), leaving the executor to invent a location and making AC6's proving command impossible to write without guessing.
findings.location: AC6: 'The deletion log artifact (ticket, comment id, body hash, outcome) exists for the executed run.'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the 'What' checklist and AC6: 'Deletion log written to artifacts/jira-cleanup/deletions-<ts>.jsonl (one JSON line per deletion attempt: ticket, comment_id, body_hash, outcome).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a unit test for the pagination completeness guard: mock `get_comments` returning N items while the metadata call reports N+1 total, and assert the script raises/exits before backup or deletion; add a complementary case where counts match and assert the run proceeds.
evidence: "reconciles len(fetched) == total per ticket, REFUSING to proceed if they differ (guards a silently-truncated fetch producing an incomplete/dangerous delete list)"
AC item 1 specifies operator-attested log-line verification only; no unit test for the truncation-detection abort path is specified
The two unit tests in AC items 3 and 4 do not cover this path
findings.finding: The pagination completeness check — `len(fetched) == total` where `total` comes from a separate `GET /rest/api/3/issue/{key}/comment?maxResults=0` call — is a new safety-critical branching behavior (script refuses to proceed if they differ), but no unit test is specified for this branch. A bug in this check (e.g., off-by-one, wrong JSON key, or the check being skipped) would allow the script to proceed with a silently-truncated fetch and generate an incomplete/dangerous delete list.
findings.location: Concrete mechanisms section — 'Pagination' paragraph; Acceptance Criteria item 1
scenarios: A bug in the metadata-call JSON parsing (wrong key name) causes `total` to always equal `len(fetched)`, silently bypassing the completeness guard
An off-by-one in the comparison allows a fetch missing one comment to pass, potentially including that comment in the keep-set instead of the delete-set

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend AC3) to verify that the secondary accountId cross-check fires: the script asserts all RECONCILER_MARKER-bearing comments resolve to a single bot accountId, and a unit/integration test confirms the run aborts when a marked comment carries an unexpected accountId.
evidence: 'the script asserts that all marked (candidate) comments across the 13 tickets resolve to a single bot accountId and records it — a divergent accountId on a marked comment aborts the run for manual review (defense in depth; the marker still governs)' — Mechanisms section
ABSENCE: no AC references the accountId assertion, the abort condition, or a test that the run halts on a divergent accountId
findings.finding: The secondary accountId cross-check behavior — 'the script asserts that all marked (candidate) comments across the 13 tickets resolve to a single bot accountId and records it — a divergent accountId on a marked comment aborts the run for manual review' — is described as a safety feature in Mechanisms but has no corresponding AC to verify it was implemented and fires correctly.
findings.location: Concrete mechanisms > Bot-authored identification (secondary cross-check paragraph) vs Acceptance Criteria (all six ACs)
scenarios: An implementer skips the accountId cross-check (it has no AC to fail); a compromised or renamed bot account is silently treated as a deletion candidate with no abort.
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC3 with a sub-clause: '(e) a divergent accountId on any marked comment triggers an abort before any deletion, verified by a unit test that injects a marked comment with a foreign accountId and asserts SystemExit / raised exception.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a unit test for the accountId-divergence abort path: assert that when candidate comments resolve to >1 distinct accountId, the script raises/exits before any deletion, and assert that a single consistent accountId passes the check — covering both the abort and the pass branch of this safety gate.
evidence: "a divergent accountId on a marked comment aborts the run for manual review (defense in depth; the marker still governs)"
AC item 3 specifies the filter unit test but only covers (a)-(d): marked-only, keep-oldest, never-single-copy, never-unmarked — does NOT enumerate the accountId divergence abort case
No unit test for the accountId cross-check is called out anywhere in What or AC sections
findings.finding: The accountId-divergence abort path — where a marked comment resolves to an unexpected bot accountId and the run is aborted for manual review — is a new safety-critical branching behavior with no corresponding unit test specified anywhere in the plan. If this guard is broken (either fails to abort or aborts spuriously on valid data), the error would be silent until a manual run, potentially allowing deletion to proceed on a corrupt or misidentified comment set.
findings.location: Concrete mechanisms section — 'Secondary cross-check' paragraph; Acceptance Criteria — no test for this branch
scenarios: A bug in accountId collection logic (e.g., set construction error) causes the guard to never fire, silently passing a run with misidentified comments
A display-name change causes the accountId set to unexpectedly contain two entries, aborting a valid run with no diagnostic output
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the AC item 3 unit test (or add a sibling test) with a synthetic comment set where one marked comment carries a different accountId; assert the script aborts pre-deletion. Add a complementary case where all marked comments share one accountId and assert the run proceeds to the dry-run/delete phase.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit enforcement mechanism to the approval gate — e.g., require `--execute --confirmed` (where `--confirmed` is only documented after dry-run review), or have the script prompt `Type 'DELETE 13 tickets' to confirm:` interactively, or require a `--dry-run-artifact=<path>` argument to --execute that proves a dry-run was completed — so --execute cannot fire without an operator actively acknowledging the diff.
evidence: '--execute is required to delete and runs ONLY after explicit human approval of the dry-run diff'
AC item: 'Dry-run diff (delete-id list + kept-oldest-id per distinct body, per ticket) is produced and human-approved before --execute. [operator-attested]'
The approval is marked [operator-attested] with no script-level enforcement described — the script does not validate that a dry-run artifact was reviewed before --execute is accepted.
ABSENCE: no --approved-by, --confirm, or interactive prompt mechanism is described anywhere in the plan.
findings.finding: The approval gate between --dry-run and --execute lacks any enforcement mechanism. The plan states '--execute is required to delete and runs ONLY after explicit human approval of the dry-run diff' but never specifies how that approval is recorded or enforced — no --approved-by flag, no interactive y/n confirmation prompt, no file-based gate. An operator could run --execute directly without reviewing the dry-run diff, bypassing the only human-safety checkpoint before ~64k+ irreversible deletions.
findings.location: What — irreversible; execute ONLY under this T4 safety plan / Dry-run default bullet; AC: 'Dry-run diff … is produced and human-approved before --execute. [operator-attested]'
scenarios: Operator under time pressure runs `python cleanup.py --execute` directly, skipping dry-run review; the script accepts the flag without any guard and proceeds to delete ~64k comments across 13 tickets irreversibly before the operator realizes the diff was never reviewed.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a mandatory interactive confirmation prompt when --execute is passed: print the dry-run summary, then require the operator to type a confirmation phrase (e.g. 'DELETE 13 TICKETS') before any deletion begins. Alternatively, require `--execute --dry-run-artifact=<path>` where the path must point to a previously written dry-run JSON, proving the dry-run was run and the artifact was produced.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the DELETE-loop retry implementation fork: specify which route to use — either 'wrap `client.delete_comment` with `_call_with_retry(client.delete_comment, jira_key, comment_id)`' (reuse the existing tested wrapper, no new code) OR 'write a new script-level wrapper' — and remove the other option so the executor has a single unambiguous decision.
evidence: Plan text: 'the cleanup script MUST supply its own 429/5xx retry wrapper around each DELETE (new script-level work): on HTTP 429 or 5xx, sleep Retry-After (or exponential backoff if absent) and retry up to a bounded count; alternatively route delete_comment through dispatch_one._call_with_retry'

dispatch_one._call_with_retry confirmed at dispatch_one.py:104 — handles urllib.error.HTTPError 429/5xx with Retry-After and jittered backoff (lines 142-163), exactly the behavior the plan describes for the new wrapper
delete_comment at acli_graph.py:546 calls _direct_rest_delete which routes through _rest_urlopen_with_retry (acli_rest.py:319) — confirmed NOT to retry 429/5xx (acli_rest.py:62-64)
Both routes satisfy the stated requirement; plan provides no selection criterion (cost, testability, coupling) to resolve the fork
findings.finding: The plan specifies TWO co-equal, mutually exclusive implementation routes for the DELETE-loop 429/5xx retry layer — a new bespoke script-level wrapper ('the cleanup script MUST supply its own 429/5xx retry wrapper — new script-level work') AND an existing one ('alternatively route delete_comment through dispatch_one._call_with_retry') — without a decision criterion for which to implement. This is a T8(b) processing-protocol gap: the decision rule governing which retry path governs the DELETE loop is absent, forcing the executor to make an arbitrary architectural choice between new untested code and a battle-tested existing wrapper.
findings.location: Concrete mechanisms section, 'Rate limiting + transient-failure handling' paragraph
scenarios: Executor implements new bespoke wrapper, duplicating logic already battle-tested in _call_with_retry, introducing a new untested code path for a safety-critical operation on 64k+ irreversible deletions.
Executor routes through _call_with_retry but must wrap delete_comment as a callable with the right signature — a detail the plan omits — leading to incorrect invocation.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the dual-option paragraph with a single directive: 'Route each DELETE through the existing `_call_with_retry(client.delete_comment, jira_key, comment_id)` (dispatch_one.py:104); this provides 429+Retry-After, 5xx jittered backoff, and exhaustion semantics with no new code. Insert a per-call `time.sleep(0.1)` throttle before the call for the ≤10 req/s budget.' This eliminates the bespoke-wrapper path entirely.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete restore procedure to the rollback section: either a `--restore` mode in the cleanup script that reads `backup-<ts>.json` and re-POSTs each comment body, or a documented curl/Python snippet an operator can run; the procedure must be executable without writing new code.
evidence: 'if a comment must be recovered, re-POST its body to the ticket (note: re-created comments get NEW ids/timestamps — content-restore, not id-restore)'
No restore script, curl template, or script invocation is specified — an operator discovering data loss must write new code to execute the rollback.
The plan elsewhere mandates a backup artifact but provides no tooling to consume it for restore.
findings.finding: The rollback plan is verbally described ('re-POST its body to the ticket') but no restore script, command template, or concrete operator procedure is provided, making the back-out path notional rather than executable.
findings.location: Rollback (irreversible-op mitigation) section
scenarios: An operator discovers a wrongly-deleted comment after --execute; they have the backup JSON but no prescribed command or script to re-POST it — rollback is blocked until they write ad-hoc code.
Partial restore under time pressure (e.g., business escalation on a capped ticket) is error-prone without a concrete procedure.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `--restore backup-<ts>.json --ticket REB-XXX` subcommand (or standalone restore script) that iterates the backup artifact for the named ticket and re-POSTs each comment body via the Jira REST API, logging each re-POSTed id. Document that restored comments receive new ids/timestamps (content-restore only).

findings.finding: The plan describes a rollback path ('re-POST its body to the ticket') but does not assert the TARGET STATE of Jira after a rollback, as required by T11. Specifically: after a partial execution that deleted N comments before aborting, re-POSTing all backed-up comments from those tickets would push them back toward or to the 5000-comment cap — re-creating the very problem being solved. The plan does not define (a) WHICH comments should be re-posted on rollback (all? only the deleted ones?), (b) what the acceptable post-rollback comment count is, or (c) how to determine which backed-up comments are still present vs already deleted, making a safe selective restore operationally undefined.
findings.location: ## What — irreversible; execute ONLY under this T4 safety plan / 'Rollback (irreversible-op mitigation)' sub-section; AC: 'Deletion log artifact'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a rollback procedure section: 'On partial-run abort, use the deletion log (ticket + deleted comment ids) to identify exactly which comments were deleted. Re-POST only those deleted comments (body from backup JSON, matched by id). Expected post-rollback state: comment count = pre-run total minus already-deleted count; if pre-run total was 5000, post-rollback count will be above the point reached mid-run. Verify with GET /comment?maxResults=0 total field. Do NOT re-POST the full backup — that would return the ticket to 5000.' Also add a post-rollback AC: 'After rollback, each affected ticket has comment count = (pre-run total) − (count in deletion log for that ticket), confirmed by metadata call.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Revise the rate-limiting approach to use `dispatch_one._call_with_retry` (or its re-export via `batch_dispatch._call_with_retry`) as the PRIMARY retry vehicle for each `client.delete_comment(key, id)` call — not a new custom wrapper — and remove the 'new script-level work' framing; update AC4's unit test to assert that `delete_comment` is invoked through `_call_with_retry` (matching the existing `test_applier_retry.py` pattern).
evidence: 'the cleanup script MUST supply its own 429/5xx retry wrapper around each DELETE (new script-level work)'
'alternatively route delete_comment through dispatch_one._call_with_retry'
dispatch_one.py:104-170: `_call_with_retry` handles JiraAPIError 429/5xx and urllib.error.HTTPError 429/5xx, reads Retry-After, applies ADR-0036 jitter, bounded max_retries
tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/apply/test_applier_retry.py:98-109: existing test asserts 429 retry + Retry-After honored
acli_rest.py:62-64: `_rest_urlopen_with_retry` correctly does NOT retry HTTPError — confirming `_call_with_retry` is the right layer, not `_rest_urlopen_with_retry`
The plan's section header says 'reused not reinvented' but the primary path re-invents what `_call_with_retry` already provides
findings.finding: The plan specifies building a new script-level 429/5xx retry wrapper for the DELETE loop as the primary approach ('the cleanup script MUST supply its own 429/5xx retry wrapper — new script-level work'), while offering `dispatch_one._call_with_retry` only as a secondary 'alternatively'. This inverts the codebase-alignment preference: `_call_with_retry` (dispatch_one.py:104-170) already implements 429+Retry-After honoring, 5xx jittered exponential backoff (ADR-0036), bounded retries, and is verified by existing tests (test_applier_retry.py). Implementing a custom wrapper introduces new, untested code where a proven facility already exists — contradicting the plan's own 'reused not reinvented' principle stated in the section header.
findings.location: Concrete mechanisms section — 'Rate limiting + transient-failure handling' paragraph; also AC4
scenarios: Implementer follows the primary path and writes a custom retry wrapper that misses an edge case (e.g. missing Retry-After header fallback, wrong backoff cap) — introducing a subtle rate-limit bug in a ~63,700-DELETE run.
The custom wrapper diverges from ADR-0036 jitter, creating inconsistent retry behavior vs. all other Jira writes in the codebase.
AC4's unit test for 429 retry tests the new custom wrapper, leaving `_call_with_retry`'s already-tested path unused and the custom code under-validated relative to the existing test suite.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'the cleanup script MUST supply its own 429/5xx retry wrapper (new script-level work)' with: 'Route each `client.delete_comment(key, id)` call through `_call_with_retry(client.delete_comment, key, id)` (from `dispatch_one` / `batch_dispatch`) — the existing ADR-0036-compliant facility that already handles 429+Retry-After and 5xx jitter, confirmed by `test_applier_retry.py`. No new retry code required.' Remove the 'alternatively' framing.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Commit to routing the DELETE loop through the existing `dispatch_one._call_with_retry` (wrapping `client.delete_comment`) as the PRIMARY retry path, not a custom wrapper — drop the 'new script-level work' framing and remove the 'alternatively' hedge.
evidence: Plan: 'the cleanup script MUST supply its own 429/5xx retry wrapper around each DELETE (new script-level work): on HTTP 429 or 5xx, sleep Retry-After (or exponential backoff if absent) and retry up to a bounded count; alternatively route delete_comment through dispatch_one._call_with_retry'

dispatch_one.py:104-165: _call_with_retry handles JiraAPIError 429/5xx, urllib.error.HTTPError 429/5xx, Retry-After header, ADR-0036 jitter, bounded max_retries — identical to what the plan's custom wrapper would implement
The 'alternatively' framing inverts the Rule of Three: prior art fully satisfies the need; a custom wrapper adds maintenance surface for no benefit
findings.finding: The plan specifies a bespoke 429/5xx retry wrapper as the PRIMARY DELETE-loop mechanism ('the cleanup script MUST supply its own 429/5xx retry wrapper — new script-level work') while listing `dispatch_one._call_with_retry` only as an 'alternatively' option. Code inspection confirms `dispatch_one._call_with_retry` (lines 104-175) already implements exactly the specified behaviour: 429+Retry-After (with `parse_retry_after`), 5xx with ADR-0036 jittered exponential backoff, bounded retries, and raw HTTPError propagation. Building a custom wrapper is repackaging existing prior art; the plan should commit to `_call_with_retry` as the primary path rather than treating it as an afterthought.
findings.location: Concrete mechanisms section — 'Rate limiting + transient-failure handling'
scenarios: Implementor writes a custom wrapper that diverges subtly from _call_with_retry (e.g. missing the parse_retry_after path, or different backoff caps), introducing a maintenance split and a latent correctness gap.
A future change to retry logic in dispatch_one is not propagated to the cleanup script's custom wrapper, silently diverging.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'the cleanup script MUST supply its own 429/5xx retry wrapper (new script-level work)' with 'route each `client.delete_comment(key, id)` call through `dispatch_one._call_with_retry(client.delete_comment, key, id)` — this already implements 429+Retry-After, 5xx jitter (ADR-0036), and bounded retries, matching exactly what is needed here.'

evidence: 1 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
vague/subjective terms present: clean, robust
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

evidence: file_impact is empty
findings.finding: No file_impact declared on a leaf work ticket.
findings.suggested_fix: Record the {path, reason} files this work will touch (e.g. via set_file_impact) so the attestation is scoped to them.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Revise the rate-limit retry spec to lead with `_call_with_retry` reuse as the PRIMARY path (wrap `client.delete_comment` calls via `_call_with_retry(client.delete_comment, key, comment_id)`) and remove the 'new script-level work' framing for the retry wrapper.
evidence: Plan: 'the cleanup script MUST supply its own 429/5xx retry wrapper around each DELETE (new script-level work)'
Plan: 'alternatively route delete_comment through dispatch_one._call_with_retry' — mentioned as secondary alternative

`dispatch_one._call_with_retry` (dispatch_one.py:104-177): handles JiraAPIError 429/5xx, urllib.error.HTTPError 429/5xx, honors Retry-After, jittered exponential backoff, bounded retries — exactly the spec
17+ call-sites grep: apply_outbound.py, apply_inbound.py, apply_inbound_records.py, dispatch_one.py, batch_dispatch.py — well above Rule-of-Three
findings.finding: The plan's primary framing for rate-limit retry is 'the script MUST supply its own 429/5xx retry wrapper (new script-level work)' — but `dispatch_one._call_with_retry` already implements exactly this (429 with Retry-After honor, 5xx with jittered exponential backoff, bounded retries) and has 17+ production call-sites. The plan buries the reuse option ('alternatively route delete_comment through dispatch_one._call_with_retry') as a secondary alternative rather than the primary path, risking the implementor building a duplicate retry engine.
findings.location: Concrete mechanisms — 'Rate limiting + transient-failure handling' section
scenarios: Implementor writes a custom retry loop that diverges from the codebase's backoff constants (MAX_BACKOFF_S=60s, ADR-0036 jitter shape), creating a second retry policy to maintain.
Custom wrapper omits the HTTPError re-raise semantics (dispatch_one.py:170-171) that downstream catchers depend on.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'the cleanup script MUST supply its own 429/5xx retry wrapper (new script-level work)' with 'route each DELETE through `dispatch_one._call_with_retry(client.delete_comment, key, comment_id)` — this already handles 429/Retry-After, 5xx/jitter, and bounded retries per ADR-0036; no new retry logic needed.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit sequencing note specifying whether the reconciler should be paused during --execute (or affirming it is safe to run concurrently), and clarify the post-execution AC evaluation timing (e.g., 'verify cap after one full reconciler cycle post-cleanup').
evidence: 'Sequencing: run ONLY after P1 is live in the reconciler' — no mention of pausing/quiescing the reconciler during --execute
'Post-execution, each of the 13 tickets is below the 5000 cap AND a reconcile pass emits 0 comment re-adds for them'
ABSENCE: no analysis of reconciler concurrency during the multi-hour delete window
findings.finding: The plan does not specify whether the live reconciler should be quiesced (paused or have its 13-ticket write path temporarily blocked) during the --execute window. While P1 prevents re-inflation of duplicates, the reconciler could legitimately add new distinct comments to these tickets during the potentially multi-hour delete loop. The delete list is computed once at the start of --execute from a fresh paginated fetch; new comments added by the reconciler mid-run will not appear in the delete list (safe from accidental deletion), but the plan's final AC ('each of the 13 tickets is below the 5000 cap') could be transiently violated if the reconciler adds comments faster than cleanup removes them on a ticket near 5000.
findings.location: Sequencing section; post-execution AC bullet
scenarios: Reconciler fires every N minutes; during a 3-hour delete loop on REB-155 (4924 deletions), reconciler adds 80+ new distinct comments; ticket stays near or above 4990; the post-execution cap check may not hold until cleanup is complete.

Reconciler adds a comment whose normalized body matches one being deleted mid-loop; the 'keep-one' grouping from the initial fetch doesn't include the new comment, so two copies of that body survive post-run — not dangerous, but means a second cleanup pass is needed.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare the required Jira permission scope for the DELETE path (e.g., 'Delete All Comments' project permission or equivalent) and add a pre-flight probe step that verifies the operator token holds that permission on each of the 13 affected projects BEFORE --execute fires.
evidence: Plan is silent on permission scope: no mention of 'Delete All Comments', 'Delete Any Comment', or project-admin requirement for the DELETE path.
Existing token pattern: `JIRA_API_TOKEN` is env-only (rebar.toml:66), used by the reconciler for read/write sync — but DELETE-comment is a separate Jira permission class.

_direct_rest_delete raises HTTPError on 4xx (acli_rest.py:62-64), so a 403 would surface as an exception — BUT only if the script's 429/5xx retry wrapper also propagates 403 (plan says 'retry 429/5xx', which implies 4xx excluding 429 are re-raised; 403 would abort the run, not silently skip). The plan does not include a pre-flight permission probe to catch this before --execute fires against all 13 tickets.
ABSENCE: no step in the operator flow says 'verify the token has delete-comment permission on these projects before executing'.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new destructive boundary-crossing path (DELETE /rest/api/3/issue/{key}/comment/{id} to Jira Cloud) but never declares what Jira permission the operator token must hold to execute it, nor whether the existing reconciler token already carries that permission. In Jira Cloud, deleting any comment requires the 'Delete All Comments' project permission (or site-admin scope) — distinct from the read/write permission used for normal sync. If the token lacks it, all ~64k DELETEs will silently 403 during --execute, producing the appearance of a successful run with zero actual deletions; if the operator elevates the token to admin-for-convenience, that violates least-privilege for a one-off script.
findings.location: Concrete mechanisms / Rate limiting section; 'What' safety plan — pre-deletion backup and operator flow steps
scenarios: Operator runs --execute with the reconciler's read/write token; token lacks 'Delete All Comments' permission; the script aborts on the first DELETE with an unhandled 403 HTTPError mid-run, leaving a partial deletion state and a consumed backup artifact.
Operator grants the token site-admin scope to make the deletions work, violating least-privilege and leaving an over-privileged token in the environment long-term.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a pre-flight check in the script (runs before backup fetch and before --execute): issue `GET /rest/api/3/mypermissions?issueKey={key}&permissions=DELETE_ALL_COMMENTS` for one representative ticket and assert the returned `havePermission` is `true`; abort with a clear message if not. Document in the plan that the operator token must hold 'Delete All Comments' (or site-admin) and that a separate, scoped token should be provisioned for this one-off operation rather than reusing the reconciler's long-lived read/write token.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either cite Jira Cloud's published REST API rate-limit policy for DELETE operations (and assert the 10 req/s default is derived from it) or include a brief empirical probe: run 50–100 DELETEs against a low-value ticket and record the first 429 Retry-After value to calibrate the default before the full run.
evidence: 'Throttle to a conservative default (≤10 req/s, i.e. ≥100ms inter-call sleep)'
No citation of Jira Cloud's published rate-limit policy or observed Retry-After headers for this tenant.
64k+ DELETEs is not a workload the existing codebase has exercised; the existing retry path explicitly does NOT handle 429 (plan-acknowledged), confirming this is uncharted territory.
findings.finding: The 10 req/s DELETE rate limit is an asserted heuristic with no derivation from Jira Cloud's published limits or an empirical probe, and the actual throughput for bulk DELETEs (~64k requests) against this tenant is unknown.
findings.location: Rate limiting + transient-failure handling section
scenarios: Jira Cloud's actual per-tenant DELETE rate limit is lower than 10 req/s, causing sustained 429 storms that halt the run and leave the tickets in a partially-cleaned state.
Alternatively the limit is much higher, making the run unnecessarily slow (hours instead of minutes) with no path to tune it without re-reading the code.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify (via a live probe or Jira Cloud v3 API docs citation) that `GET /rest/api/3/issue/{key}/comment?maxResults=0` returns a reliable `total` field equal to the full comment count, and record the verification result in the plan before implementation.
evidence: Plan: 'GET /rest/api/3/issue/{key}/comment?maxResults=0 returns {total, maxResults, startAt, comments: []}; the script reads total from that envelope'
No usage of ?maxResults=0 comment endpoint appears anywhere in src/ or scripts/ — this is an unverified API capability assertion

The existing _parse_paginated_comments path (the reuse target) explicitly discards the response envelope, so total is not available from it — hence the need for a separate call, but the separate call's contract is unverified
findings.finding: The plan asserts that `GET /rest/api/3/issue/{key}/comment?maxResults=0` returns an envelope `{total, maxResults, startAt, comments: []}` and treats `total` from that envelope as the authoritative completeness check — but this specific zero-page metadata call pattern is not used or verified anywhere in the existing codebase. The plan labels this as 'new script-level work, not provided by the reuse target', but does not cite a Jira Cloud API reference or probe result confirming that `?maxResults=0` returns a truthful `total` (as opposed to returning 0 or omitting the field).
findings.location: Concrete mechanisms section — 'Pagination (fetch ALL 5000, not the first page)'
scenarios: Jira Cloud returns total=0 or omits the total field for ?maxResults=0 (some Jira versions cap or ignore maxResults=0 differently), causing the completeness check to always pass or always fail, defeating the safety gate.
The ?maxResults=0 call returns the total of a single page rather than the global total, making the guard unreliable for >50-comment tickets.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a live probe confirming `GET /rest/api/3/issue/{key}/comment?maxResults=0` returns a `total` field matching Jira's true comment count for at least one of the 13 capped tickets (e.g. REB-155 with known total 5000) before committing to this as the completeness-check mechanism.
evidence: Plan text: 'GET /rest/api/3/issue/{key}/comment?maxResults=0 returns {total, maxResults, startAt, comments: []}; the script reads total from that envelope (new script-level work, not provided by the reuse target)'

No repo code calls /rest/api/3/issue/{key}/comment with maxResults=0 — this surface is unexercised in the codebase (search confirmed only comment_map.json fixtures show the base /comment path without query params)
Evidence class: PARTIALLY-VERIFIED — Jira Cloud REST v3 comment list is a known endpoint, but the maxResults=0 -> {total} response shape is unconfirmed in this environment
Safety consequence: the completeness check is the primary guard preventing deletion from a truncated comment set; an incorrect total breaks the gate
findings.finding: The completeness-check safety gate depends on `GET /rest/api/3/issue/{key}/comment?maxResults=0` returning a `total` field in the response envelope. This specific endpoint surface — using `maxResults=0` as a lightweight count probe — is new to this codebase and has not been live-probed. If Jira Cloud returns `total: 0` (interpreting `maxResults=0` as 'no results requested, report 0') or omits `total` when `comments` is empty, the completeness check silently passes with an incorrect total, allowing a truncated fetch to proceed to deletion. The plan calls this 'new script-level work' but does not require a prior live probe or spike to confirm the response shape.
findings.location: Concrete mechanisms section — 'Pagination' paragraph: 'GET /rest/api/3/issue/{key}/comment?maxResults=0 returns {total, maxResults, startAt, comments: []}; the script reads total from that envelope'
scenarios: Jira returns total:0 when maxResults=0 is passed, misinterpreting the parameter as a limit that truncates the count — completeness check passes spuriously

Jira omits total from the envelope when the response comments array is empty — script raises KeyError or proceeds with total=0, comparing against len(fetched)=5000 and refusing correctly, or crashes before any delete
A Jira Cloud tenant configuration or API version difference causes the envelope shape to differ from documented behavior
findings.suggested_fix: Run `curl -u user:token 'https://<instance>.atlassian.net/rest/api/3/issue/REB-155/comment?maxResults=0' | python -m json.tool | grep total` against the live instance and record the response shape in the plan. Alternatively use `_direct_rest_get('/rest/api/3/issue/REB-155/comment?maxResults=1')` (maxResults=1 returns at most 1 comment but still reports total) as a less ambiguous probe — this avoids the maxResults=0 edge case entirely.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit instruction (and ideally a .gitignore entry for `artifacts/jira-cleanup/`) to the plan's operator flow, stating that backup and deletion-log artifacts must not be committed to version control.
evidence: Plan specifies backup path: 'artifacts/jira-cleanup/backup-<ts>.json'
Backup contains: 'id, body, author.accountId, author.displayName, created' — displayName is user PII.
ABSENCE: no mention of .gitignore entry, --exclude, or operator instruction to not commit the artifact.
The repo's existing .gitignore discipline (tickets branch gitignore, not main-branch) does not automatically cover a new top-level artifacts/ directory.
findings.finding: The backup artifact `artifacts/jira-cleanup/backup-<ts>.json` contains full ADF comment bodies and `author.displayName` (user PII) for up to 65,000 comments across 13 tickets. The plan does not state that this path must be excluded from version control (e.g., via `.gitignore`), creating a risk that a routine `git add -A` during or after the cleanup inadvertently commits 65k user-attributed comments into the repo's permanent history.
findings.location: What — Pre-deletion backup step; Acceptance Criteria AC1
scenarios: Operator runs git add -A to stage cleanup script and accidentally stages the 65k-comment backup JSON, committing user display names and full comment content into the public repo history — irreversible without a git history rewrite.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `artifacts/jira-cleanup/` to the repo-root `.gitignore` (or the main-branch `.gitignore`) before running the script, and include this as a mandatory pre-step in the operator flow.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Expose `--rate-limit` (requests/sec, default 10) and `--max-retries` (default e.g. 5) as explicit CLI parameters so operators can tune them without modifying source; document the defaults and their derivation in the script's `--help` output.
evidence: 'Throttle to a conservative default (≤10 req/s)' — 'default' implies configurability but no CLI flag is named.
'retry up to a bounded count' — the bound is unspecified and not listed as a configurable parameter.
These are operational thresholds expected to require tuning (T5e: 'rules/thresholds expected to evolve are configurable, not hardcoded').
findings.finding: The rate-limit threshold (10 req/s) and retry count ('bounded count') are described as defaults in prose but the plan does not specify them as CLI-configurable parameters, leaving operators with no hook to adjust them based on observed Jira Cloud behavior without modifying the script.
findings.location: Rate limiting + transient-failure handling section; Acceptance Criteria — Rate-limit safety
scenarios: First operator run hits 429 storms at 10 req/s; they must edit the script source to reduce the rate rather than passing --rate-limit 5.
A future cleanup of a different inflated ticket needs a different retry count; same problem.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `argparse` arguments: `--rate-limit FLOAT` (default=10, help='Max DELETE requests per second') and `--max-retries INT` (default=5, help='Max retries on 429/5xx before aborting').

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend the new DELETE retry wrapper to catch and retry transport-level exceptions (socket timeout, `urllib.error.URLError`, connection reset) with the same backoff/bounded-retry logic as HTTP 429/5xx, and log the exception type and retry count on each attempt.
evidence: 'the cleanup script MUST supply its own 429/5xx retry wrapper around each DELETE (new script-level work): on HTTP 429 or 5xx, sleep Retry-After (or exponential backoff if absent) and retry up to a bounded count'
ABSENCE: no mention of socket timeout, URLError, or connection-reset handling in the new wrapper spec
'the existing _direct_rest_delete -> _rest_urlopen_with_retry path does NOT retry HTTP 429/5xx — it re-raises 4xx/5xx immediately'
findings.finding: The DELETE retry wrapper is specified to handle HTTP 429 and 5xx responses, but the plan does not address transport-layer / connection-level errors (socket timeouts, connection resets, `urllib.error.URLError`) that can occur over a ~64k-DELETE loop. Since the plan explicitly bypasses the existing `_rest_urlopen_with_retry` and requires a new script-level wrapper, these non-HTTP exceptions would propagate as unhandled errors, aborting the run mid-stream without a clear resume signal in the log.
findings.location: Rate limiting + transient-failure handling section; AC 'Rate-limit safety' bullet
scenarios: A flaky network or Jira Cloud hiccup causes a connection reset on DELETE #12,000 of ~64k; the run aborts; the log shows the last successful delete but not why it stopped; operator must manually inspect.
A socket timeout (no HTTP response) mid-run silently halts iteration without incrementing the retry counter.
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap the DELETE call in a try/except that catches both `urllib.error.HTTPError` (for 429/5xx) and `urllib.error.URLError` / `socket.timeout` (for transport errors), applies the same exponential-backoff-with-Retry-After logic, and logs `{ticket, comment_id, attempt, error_type, status_or_reason}` before each retry.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that `dispatch_one._call_with_retry` actually exists in `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/dispatch_one.py`; if not, remove the 'alternatively route through dispatch_one._call_with_retry' option from the plan and confirm the cleanup script supplies its own 429/5xx retry wrapper as the only path.
evidence: Search for 'dispatch_one._call_with_retry' returns only cache/plan-review JSON files, not source code
No hits for '_call_with_retry' in src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/dispatch_one.py appear in grep results
The plan presents this as an existing, named alternative ('alternatively route ... through dispatch_one._call_with_retry') implying the symbol already exists
findings.finding: The plan cites `dispatch_one._call_with_retry` as an existing symbol the cleanup script could route deletions through for 429/5xx retry, but this symbol cannot be found in the repo via search.
findings.location: Concrete mechanisms / Rate limiting section: 'alternatively route `delete_comment` through `dispatch_one._call_with_retry`'
scenarios: If the implementer chooses the 'route through dispatch_one._call_with_retry' path (the plan's alternative), they will discover the symbol does not exist and need to write the retry wrapper from scratch — the plan's alternative option is a hallucinated shortcut.
findings.suggested_fix: Search dispatch_one.py for `_call_with_retry`; if absent, remove the alternative clause and clarify the script must implement its own wrapper (consistent with the 'new script-level work' statement already in the plan).

findings.finding: The plan specifies the safety-critical bot-author filter as checking whether a comment's 'raw body contains the reconciler marker' — but Jira REST returns comment bodies as ADF dicts, not plain strings, so a direct substring search for RECONCILER_MARKER on the raw body field will fail for every ADF-stored comment. The correct approach (used in inbound_differ.py:457) is to check RECONCILER_MARKER in the ADF-normalized text (after adf_to_text conversion), which is what _normalize_comment_body already does. If implemented literally against the raw ADF dict, the marker filter silently reports no bot-authored comments and the dedup produces an empty delete list — the cleanup does nothing, or worse, if body-type assumptions differ, mislabels comments.
findings.location: Concrete mechanisms — 'Bot-authored identification (safety-critical filter)' section
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'its raw body contains the reconciler marker' with 'its normalized text body (after _normalize_comment_body / adf_to_text) contains RECONCILER_MARKER — checked on the text output of _normalize_comment_body before the marker-strip step, or equivalently via a pre-strip version of the function'. Note: _normalize_comment_body strips the marker after detecting it is the normalization key; the detection step should use `adf_to_text(body)` or a dedicated predicate that checks the text form.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Require the backup writer to write to a temp file in the same directory (e.g., `backup-<ts>.json.tmp`), fsync, then atomically rename to `backup-<ts>.json` — so the target file is either absent or complete, and the completeness check only runs on a fully-renamed file.
evidence: 'export FULL comment payload … to a versioned JSON artifact under artifacts/jira-cleanup/backup-<ts>.json BEFORE any deletion'
'the script asserts, per ticket, len(backup[key]) == jira_reported_total and that the artifact is non-empty and parses'
ABSENCE: no write-to-temp-then-rename or fsync requirement specified
findings.finding: The backup JSON write has no specified atomicity guarantee. If the process is interrupted (SIGKILL, OOM, disk-full) during the write of a large multi-ticket backup file, the result is a partially-written or syntactically invalid JSON file. The guard — `python -m json.tool` parse check + per-ticket count reconciliation — runs AFTER the write and correctly rejects an invalid file, but a partial write that happens to be syntactically valid JSON (e.g., truncated mid-array but still closes brackets) could pass the parse check while silently omitting some comments, allowing deletions to proceed against an incomplete backup.
findings.location: Pre-deletion backup AC bullet; 'Backup artifact exists and passes its per-ticket completeness check' AC
scenarios: Disk full at 80% through writing the backup causes truncation; the resulting JSON is parseable (truncated in the middle of the last ticket's array, accidentally closed by a prior brace); per-ticket count check catches this — but only if the truncation cut whole comment objects; a truncation mid-field could produce a shorter-than-expected but valid JSON list.
SIGKILL mid-write leaves a corrupt file; subsequent run cannot distinguish it from a completed backup without the atomic-rename guard.
findings.suggested_fix: Use Python's `tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile(dir=target_dir, delete=False)`, write and flush/fsync, then `os.replace(tmp_path, final_path)` — `os.replace` is atomic on POSIX and provides the rename guarantee.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the script calls `client.delete_comment(jira_key, comment_id)` (acli_graph.py:546) rather than constructing raw DELETE requests, to reuse the probe-validated encapsulation.
evidence: Plan: 'delete the rest via DELETE /rest/api/3/issue/{key}/comment/{id}'

`AcliClient.delete_comment(jira_key, comment_id)` at acli_graph.py:546-554: 'ACLI has no comment delete subcommand. Uses direct REST API: DELETE /rest/api/3/issue/{key}/comment/{id}. Probe-validated: returns 204 on success.'
No mention of `delete_comment` method anywhere in the plan text
findings.finding: The plan specifies raw `DELETE /rest/api/3/issue/{key}/comment/{id}` calls without mentioning `AcliClient.delete_comment` (acli_graph.py:546-554), which already encapsulates exactly this REST call — probe-validated, returns 204 on success. The script would need to either re-implement the credential/URL construction or call `_direct_rest_delete` directly on the mixin, bypassing the documented public method.
findings.location: Concrete mechanisms — 'Keep-one rule' section; AC line 'DELETE /rest/api/3/issue/{key}/comment/{id}'
scenarios: Implementor hand-rolls credential construction and URL formatting, duplicating what `_direct_rest_delete` already provides.
A future credential rotation or URL-construction change is applied to `AcliRestMixin` but not to the hand-rolled cleanup script.
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'Keep-one rule' section, replace 'delete the rest via DELETE /rest/api/3/issue/{key}/comment/{id}' with 'delete the rest via `_call_with_retry(client.delete_comment, jira_key, comment_id)` — `delete_comment` (acli_graph.py:546) is the existing probe-validated encapsulation of that endpoint.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
